Tuti
Your Interactive Tutorial Builder

An agentic AI workflow that turns a course session — a slide deck or reading page — into a guided, interactive tutorial that teaches, checks understanding, and assesses. 🎓

Agentic workflow 🤖 LangGraph · 5 agents 🧑‍🏫 Human-in-the-loop

Project showcase · What it is · Key learnings · The journey & takeaways

The problem

Our reading material is stuck in markdown.

📄

Only text to read

Markdown can't be made visually appealing — it's plain content, with no room for rich visuals or interaction.

🖼️

Visuals are painful

Adding a single image means converting it, uploading to S3, and manually linking it into the markdown — slow, and it doesn't scale.

🔁

Even tutorials are markdown

Existing tutorials already have content and MCQs — but they live in markdown too, so they carry the same limitations.

The goal: turn our markdown reading material into interactive HTML tutorials — visual, interactive, with quizzes and assessments built in. ✨

What it is

From a raw deck to a guided tutorial — in one workflow.

Upload a PPTX or HTML session (plus optional reading material). Tuti splits it into blocks, rewrites each into clean interactive content, turns concept diagrams into animations, gates progress with MCQs, and ends with an assessment.

📊
Source
PPTX / HTML + material
→
Tuti
agentic pipeline
→
🎓
Tutorial
blocks · animations · quizzes
See it — the output

A self-contained, guided learning experience.

  • 📖Progressively revealed content blocks — not a wall of text.
  • 🎬Concept diagrams become animations that build step by step.
  • 🧠Mandatory MCQs gate each step — you answer to continue.
  • 🎯A closing assessment ties the whole session together.

One self-contained HTML file — drops straight into the learning platform.

How it works

An orchestrated pipeline of specialized agents — with a human in the loop.

Automated agent 🧑‍🏫 Human gate (×6) 🧠 Memory ⚖️ Eval / self-refine 🎓 Output
The agents

Five specialists, each with one job — and its own quality bar.

🧩

Block Divider

Groups the session into 4–5 cohesive teaching blocks.

✍️

Agent 1 · Content

Rewrites each block into house-style HTML — source-only, nothing invented.

🎬

Agent 2 · Animation

A vision model recreates concept diagrams as clean, labelled animations.

🧠

Agent 3 · MCQs

Per-block quizzes that test the concept directly — material terms only.

🎯

Agent 4 · Assessment

A session-wide, descriptive wrap-up with model answers.

⚖️

The Judge

Scores every output against its rubric and drives self-correction.

Trust & quality

Six human gates, backed by machine checks.

  • 👤Review at every stage — blueprint, content, animation, MCQ, assessment & a final whole-tutorial review.
  • 🎛️Per-element control — accept, reject, or improve a single block, animation, or question.
  • 📏Eval-sets + self-validation — agents score their own output and regenerate on fail.
  • 💾Course memory — a correction is remembered and applied once, never re-asked.
Under the hood

Production-shaped, not a demo script.

6
human-in-the-loop gates
5
specialized agents
7
rubric eval-sets
2
ingestion paths · PPTX & HTML
🕸️ LangGraph orchestration 👁️ Claude Sonnet 4.6 (vision) ⚡ FastAPI + live SSE ⚛️ React + Vite UI 🗄️ Supabase persistence ↩️ Resumable checkpoints 💰 Cost tracking

Every run is persisted, resumable, and observable — pause at any gate, pick up later, and watch live cost & eval scores.

Key learnings

What building an agentic workflow actually taught us.

🎯

Fidelity beats fluency

The hard part isn't generating content — it's keeping it strictly to the source. Giving the judge the source + a "grounding pass" stopped the model from "helpfully" inventing.

🧑‍⚖️

Human gates > full autonomy

Bounded self-checks + a human at every stage produced far more trustworthy output than an unattended auto-refine loop.

📏

Evals are the spec

Rubric eval-sets turn a vague "make it good" into something measurable — and enforceable, since a low score triggers a regenerate.

⚙️

Model & prompts matter

Animation clarity and source-adherence jumped with a stronger model and a "revise this exact item" refine contract.

The journey

From idea to a working, iterated product.

🦴
Skeleton first
End-to-end pipeline on a mock LLM — wire the graph, then fill the agents.
🤖
Agents + animations
Content, MCQs, assembler, then vision-driven animations and the assessment.
🛡️
Quality & control
Eval-sets, self-validation, six HITL gates, per-element accept / reject / improve.
🚀
Memory, UI & polish
Course memory, a clean React app, and hard-won fixes: fidelity, visual animations, honoring feedback.
Key takeaways

Tuti turns any session into a guided, assessed tutorial — reliably.

  • 1The winning pattern is specialized agents + evals + human gates + memory — not one big prompt.
  • 2Quality is engineered — through grounding, rubrics, and review — not hoped for.
  • 3It's production-shaped: resumable, persisted, observable, with a clean UI.
Thank you!
